topics.name: AI & ML
topics.intro: Moonshot AI's open-source Kimi K2.7 Code headlined a coding-heavy weekend, alongside Google DeepMind's diffusion-based DiffusionGemma and a git-style /fork command for Claude Code.

items.headline: Moonshot AI releases Kimi K2.7 Code, a 1-trillion-parameter open coding model that cuts reasoning tokens 30%
items.brief: Beijing-based Moonshot AI shipped Kimi K2.7 Code, a Mixture-of-Experts model with 1 trillion total and 32 billion active parameters, under a Modified MIT license on its Kimi APIs and Hugging Face. The company reports gains over the prior K2.6 of +21.8% on Kimi Code Bench v2 (62.0 vs 50.9), +11.0% on Program Bench and +31.5% on MLS Bench Lite, with a 256K context window and roughly 30% fewer reasoning tokens spent per task.
items.whyItMatters: An openly downloadable trillion-parameter coder undercuts the cost case for closed agentic models on long-horizon software work.

items.headline: Google DeepMind open-sources DiffusionGemma, a 26B model that generates text up to 4x faster via diffusion
items.brief: DeepMind released DiffusionGemma, a 26-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model that abandons sequential token generation, instead laying out a canvas of random tokens and refining them in parallel like an image denoiser. Running on a single Nvidia H100 it reaches nearly 4x the speed of Gemma 4 26B and needs about 18GB of memory; it ships under Apache 2.0 on Hugging Face with vLLM, MLX and HF Transformers support, though it trails Gemma 4 12B on the GPQA-Diamond benchmark.
items.whyItMatters: It puts a fast, open text-diffusion model in developers' hands, validating diffusion as a practical alternative to autoregression for speed-critical work.

items.headline: Anthropic adds a /fork command to Claude Code, bringing git-style branching to AI coding sessions
items.brief: Anthropic introduced a /fork command for Claude Code that branches an existing session into a parallel variant, letting developers test an alternative approach without losing the original context — keep the fork if it works, discard it if it doesn't. The update also adds a new command-line interface, nested sub-agents that a primary session coordinates, smarter model and region handling, and a plugin search interface.
items.whyItMatters: Borrowing git's branching mental model makes parallel experimentation cheap in what Anthropic calls its fastest-growing product ever.

topics.name: Startups & Tech
topics.intro: The product and operational side of tech moved on real-world deployments — Figma and Reddit shipped new creation tools while a $365M surveillance build-out went live for the 2026 World Cup kickoff.

items.headline: Fortem, Lenovo and Boston Dynamics power a $365M surveillance grid as the 2026 World Cup kicks off
items.brief: As the tournament opened June 11 with Mexico vs. South Africa at Estadio Azteca, a $365M DHS/FEMA-backed security build-out went live across the 16 North American venues. Fortem Technologies is running kinetic counter-drone interceptors, Lenovo is building real-time 'digital twins' of stadiums, Booz Allen Hamilton is fusing drone imagery with GPS tracking, and Boston Dynamics' Spot robot dogs are patrolling Dallas and New Jersey, with facial recognition for entry and payments at Gillette, Hard Rock and Mercedes-Benz stadiums.
items.whyItMatters: It is one of the largest peacetime deployments of private surveillance and robotics tech in the US, normalizing facial recognition and autonomous patrols at mass events.

items.headline: Figma launches a Chrome extension that turns any live webpage into editable design layers
items.brief: On June 11 Figma released an official Chrome extension that captures a full webpage — or a single element like a hero section or button — and pastes it into Figma Design or FigJam as real, editable frames, text and shapes rather than a flat screenshot. The capture tools are in beta and limited to paid plans, with system-aware component generation 'coming soon.'
items.whyItMatters: It collapses the copy-from-the-web-to-redesign workflow into a paste shortcut, deepening Figma's grip on designers' starting point.

items.headline: Reddit rolls out video comments to all users globally across iOS, Android and desktop
items.brief: Reddit announced June 11 that video in comments is now live for all users worldwide, adding a video icon beside the existing image and GIF options in the comment composer so people can record or upload clips as replies. Videos don't autoplay, respect audio settings and pass through Reddit's safety systems; the feature is on in eligible SFW public communities after an alpha test in subreddits including r/popculturechat and r/fountainpens.
items.whyItMatters: It pushes Reddit further toward short-form video engagement, opening a new content and ad surface inside its highest-traffic threads.

topics.name: Markets & Finance
topics.intro: Markets were dominated by SpaceX's record-shattering Nasdaq debut that minted Elon Musk as the world's first trillionaire, while Wall Street eyed Kevin Warsh's first Fed meeting and a wave of AI mega-money.

items.headline: SpaceX closes up 19% in largest-ever IPO, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire
items.brief: SpaceX priced its Nasdaq IPO at $135 a share, opened at $150, touched $176 intraday and settled at $160.95 on June 12 — a 19% first-day pop that valued the company near $2.3 trillion. The offering raised roughly $75 billion and was oversubscribed about 4x; Elon Musk's stake pushed his net worth past $1 trillion, while early backers Founders Fund, Sequoia and Andreessen Horowitz booked tens of billions in gains and some 4,400 employees became millionaires.
items.whyItMatters: The biggest IPO in history reopens the mega-listing window and sets the bar for OpenAI and Anthropic's own anticipated trillion-dollar debuts later in 2026.

items.headline: Jeff Bezos's Prometheus raises $12B at a $41B valuation to build an 'artificial general engineer'
items.brief: Prometheus, the industrial-AI startup co-led by Jeff Bezos and Stanford scientist Vik Bajaj, announced a $12 billion Series B on June 11 at a $41 billion valuation, drawing in JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock, DST Global and Arch Venture Partners. The round lifts total funding to about $18.2 billion in roughly seven months for a 150-person firm — spread across San Francisco, London and Zurich — that aims to compress the design-to-manufacturing cycle for products from jet engines to pharmaceuticals.
items.whyItMatters: It is one of the largest private rounds in AI history and signals Wall Street's biggest banks are now writing venture-scale checks on AI applied to physical engineering, not just software.

items.headline: Altman, Amodei and Hassabis to join the G7 summit in France as AI policy moves to the world stage
items.brief: OpenAI's Sam Altman, Anthropic's Dario Amodei and Google DeepMind's Demis Hassabis will attend the G7 leaders' summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on June 15–17, the first such gathering with all three frontier-AI chiefs, with President Emmanuel Macron personally inviting Altman. Negotiators expect a package of voluntary commitments rather than binding rules, with youth safety and frontier cyber and biological risks atop the agenda; both Anthropic (around a $965 billion valuation) and OpenAI (eyeing $1 trillion-plus) have recently filed confidential IPO paperwork.
items.whyItMatters: The summit could shape the regulatory backdrop for the most valuable companies heading to public markets, determining how lightly or heavily AI's biggest money machines get governed.

items.headline: Kevin Warsh chairs his first Fed meeting June 16-17 with rates set to hold at 3.5%-3.75%
items.brief: The FOMC meets June 16–17 in Kevin Warsh's debut as Fed chair following Jerome Powell's term ending May 15, with markets pricing a 99.5% chance rates stay at 3.5%–3.75%. Persistent inflation — recent CPI near 4.2% year-over-year — has flipped the outlook so futures now lean toward a hike rather than a cut as the next move, and Warsh is expected to signal a shift away from any easing bias, with some watchers speculating he could scrap the dot-plot projections.
items.whyItMatters: A new chair leaning hawkish, with rate hikes back on the table, threatens the cheap-money assumptions underpinning this year's record equity and IPO surge.

items.headline: Oil drops as US and Iran near deal to reopen Strait of Hormuz, with WTI down to $84.88
items.brief: U.S. crude fell 3.2% to $84.88 a barrel and Brent slid 3.4% to $87.33 on June 12 as Washington and Tehran moved closer to an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, lift the U.S. naval blockade and dismantle Iran's nuclear program. A senior Trump administration official put the odds of a signed deal in coming days at about 80%, and Iran's Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi said a memorandum of understanding has 'never been closer'; prices fell roughly 6% on the week but remain more than 20% above pre-conflict levels.
items.whyItMatters: A reopened Hormuz would unwind the war-risk premium that drove crude above $100, easing inflation pressure just as the Fed weighs whether to hike.

items.headline: Wall Street rises Friday on SpaceX debut and Iran optimism; S&P 500 closes at 7,431
items.brief: U.S. stocks advanced June 12 as SpaceX's blockbuster listing lifted sentiment and investors bet on a U.S.-Iran peace deal: the S&P 500 gained 0.5% to 7,431.46, the Nasdaq Composite added 0.31% to 25,888.84, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 353.51 points, or 0.7%, to 51,202.26. The gains capped a week shadowed by hot inflation data ahead of the June 16-17 Fed meeting and a falling oil price.
items.whyItMatters: Risk appetite is holding near highs even as a hawkish Fed and sticky 4.2% inflation loom, leaving markets exposed if next week's policy signal disappoints.

topics.name: Boxing
topics.intro: Jesse 'Bam' Rodriguez's sixth-round knockout of Antonio Vargas just made him a three-division champ and fast-tracked talk of a Naoya Inoue super fight, while a loaded final stretch of June runs through Collazo-Canoy, Garner's Southampton stadium night and the June 27 Zayas-Ennis 154-pound unification in Brooklyn.

items.headline: Jesse 'Bam' Rodriguez KOs Antonio Vargas in 6 to win WBA bantamweight title, becomes three-division champ
items.brief: Rodriguez dropped Vargas in the fourth and finished him with a straight left in the sixth Saturday at Desert Diamond Arena in Glendale, Arizona, taking the WBA bantamweight belt in his 118-pound debut. The win moves Rodriguez to 24-0 (17 KOs) and makes him a champion in a third weight class; Vargas falls to 22-2-1.
items.whyItMatters: It cements Bam as boxing's premier little-man star and sets up the long-rumored Naoya Inoue showdown.

items.headline: Xander Zayas defends WBO/WBA junior middleweight titles vs Jaron 'Boots' Ennis on June 27 at Barclays Center
items.brief: Unified 154-pound champion Zayas (23-0, 13 KOs) takes on former welterweight unified king Ennis (35-0, 31 KOs), who is chasing a two-weight title run, on June 27 in Brooklyn. The bout streams on DAZN pay-per-view and pits the 22-year-old Puerto Rican against one of the sport's most avoided punchers.
items.whyItMatters: The winner becomes the clear face of a deep junior middleweight division and a two-belt unified champion.

items.headline: Oscar Collazo defends unified WBA/WBO minimumweight titles vs Joey Canoy this Saturday in Oceanside
items.brief: WBA, WBO and Ring minimumweight champion Collazo puts his unified 105-pound belts on the line against Joey Canoy on June 20 at Frontwave Arena in Oceanside, California, on a Golden Boy/Miguel Cotto Promotions card streaming on DAZN. Collazo headlines as one of the division's pound-for-pound entrants.
items.whyItMatters: A win keeps Collazo on course toward undisputed status at strawweight.

items.headline: Ryan Garner vs Michael Magnesi for WBC interim super featherweight title, June 20 at St Mary's Stadium
items.brief: Unbeaten Southampton man Garner (19-0) faces Italy's Michael Magnesi (26-2, 13 KOs) for the WBC interim super featherweight title in front of his home crowd at St Mary's Stadium on June 20, on a Queensberry card shown on DAZN. The co-feature has local light heavyweight Lewis Edmondson (11-1) meeting Lyndon Arthur (25-3) for the European and Commonwealth titles.
items.whyItMatters: It's Garner's leap to the world stage in a hometown stadium night.

items.headline: Ashton 'H2O' Sylve vs Joseph 'JoJo' Diaz headlines MF Pro's U.S. debut Friday in Long Beach
items.brief: Sylve (13-1, 10 KOs) meets former two-time world champion Joseph Diaz (34-9-1, 15 KOs) over 10 rounds at super lightweight on June 19 at Thunder Studios in Long Beach, California, headlining MF Pro's first American card on DAZN. The 22-year-old Sylve fights at home seeking a third straight win; Diaz calls it a crossroads bout.
items.whyItMatters: A statement win would push prospect Sylve toward contender status at 140.

items.headline: Naoya Inoue vs Bam Rodriguez super fight being targeted for January 2027 in Japan
items.brief: With Bam Rodriguez now a three-weight champion, a showdown with undisputed super bantamweight king Naoya Inoue (33-0) is being planned for January 2027 in Japan as part of Riyadh Season, per reports. Inoue most recently beat Junto Nakatani by unanimous decision on May 2 at the Tokyo Dome to retain all his 122-pound belts.
items.whyItMatters: It would be one of the biggest pound-for-pound fights boxing can make.

items.headline: Oshae Jones and Elia Carranza fight to split draw, Jones keeps IBF/IBO super welterweight titles in Orlando
items.brief: Champion Oshae Jones retained her IBF and IBO super welterweight belts via split draw (96-94, 94-96, 95-95) against Elia Carranza on June 13 at Caribe Royale Orlando, on the MVPW 04 card. The razor-thin result follows Jones' narrow split-decision win in their first meeting and settles nothing between them.
items.whyItMatters: A close draw all but guarantees a trilogy in a heated women's 154-pound rivalry.

items.headline: Tommy Fury outpoints Eddie Hall over six rounds in Misfits Boxing 23 heavyweight main event
items.brief: Fury beat former World's Strongest Man Eddie Hall by majority decision (59-56, 58-56, 57-57) in a six-round crossover heavyweight bout on June 13 in Manchester, England, headlining Misfits Boxing 23. The win keeps Fury unbeaten in the influencer-boxing arena.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps crossover-star Fury marketable while a return to sanctioned pro boxing stays unresolved.

topics.name: MMA
topics.intro: MMA's center of gravity is Washington this week: UFC stages its first-ever card on the White House South Lawn, headlined by Ilia Topuria defending the lightweight crown against Justin Gaethje and Alex Pereira chasing a third-division title.

items.headline: Topuria vs. Gaethje headlines UFC's White House card tonight; Pereira chases third-division title vs. Gane
items.brief: UFC Freedom 250 runs Sunday, June 14, from the South Lawn of the White House in Washington, D.C., with undefeated lightweight champion Ilia Topuria (17-0) unifying against interim champ Justin Gaethje (27-5). In the co-main, Alex Pereira — already a 185- and 205-pound titlist — moves to heavyweight (weighing 251 to Ciryl Gane's 248) to fight for the interim heavyweight belt. The card also features Sean O'Malley vs. Aiemann Zahabi and Michael Chandler vs. Mauricio Ruffy.
items.whyItMatters: A first-of-its-kind UFC event at the White House; Topuria can cement pound-for-pound supremacy while Pereira bids to become a three-division champion.

items.headline: Gabriel Bonfim shuts out former champ Belal Muhammad over five rounds at welterweight
items.brief: At UFC Fight Night on June 6 at the UFC Apex in Enterprise, Nevada, Gabriel Bonfim beat former welterweight champion Belal Muhammad by unanimous decision, sweeping all three cards 50-45. The lopsided loss marks a sharp downturn for Muhammad, who had previously held the 170-pound belt.
items.whyItMatters: A dominant win vaults Bonfim into the welterweight title picture while raising questions about where Muhammad stands in a crowded division.

items.headline: Manel Kape vs. Kyoji Horiguchi flyweight rematch headlines June 20 in Las Vegas
items.brief: UFC Fight Night: Kape vs. Horiguchi lands Saturday, June 20, at the UFC Apex in Enterprise, Nevada, with Manel Kape facing Kyoji Horiguchi in a 125-pound rematch — Horiguchi submitted Kape back at the 2017 RIZIN Grand Prix. Ion Cuțelaba vs. Navajo Stirling co-headlines at light heavyweight.
items.whyItMatters: A win keeps either flyweight in the divisional title conversation and settles a near-decade-old score from their RIZIN meeting.

items.headline: ASML's 11% Mistral AI stake set for markup as startup eyes €20bn valuation in new €3bn raise
items.brief: Mistral AI is in talks to raise about €3 billion ($3.5 billion) at a valuation near €20 billion, according to Bloomberg reporting on June 12, 2026, alongside investors including Bpifrance, Lightspeed, General Catalyst and Andreessen Horowitz. ASML, which became Mistral's largest shareholder via a €1.3 billion investment for an 11% stake at roughly an €11.7 billion valuation in September 2025, would see that holding marked up sharply if the round closes at the reported price.
items.whyItMatters: A near-doubling of Mistral's valuation would lift the carrying value of ASML's strategic AI stake well above its €1.3bn cost, reinforcing the lithography maker's bet linking its hardware to European AI.

items.headline: ServiceNow patches unauthenticated API flaw after attackers query customer instance data; first bug-bounty report came April 22
items.brief: ServiceNow disclosed on June 9 that a misconfigured REST API endpoint (/api/now/related_list_edit/create, set to require no authentication) let unauthenticated users query customer instance tables, with anomalous activity traced to June 2–3 from IP 51.159.98.241. The company shipped a fix to hosted instances on June 5 — roughly six weeks after receiving a confidential bug-bounty submission describing a similar issue on April 22 — and quietly notified affected customers via support bulletins rather than a public announcement. ServiceNow later said the observed activity was 'likely tied to security researchers or customer-led research' rather than malicious actors, has not disclosed the number of impacted customers, and the flaw has no CVE; it primarily affected instances on the Australia platform release or older releases with certain config changes.
items.whyItMatters: Customer data exposure plus a ~6-week gap between report and patch raises trust and disclosure-practice questions for a platform that holds enterprises' IT tickets, employee records and security data — watch for further regulatory or customer scrutiny.

items.headline: Visa integrates 'Intelligent Commerce' into OpenAI's models to let AI agents make tokenized payments
items.brief: On June 12, 2026, Visa said it is embedding its agentic-commerce protocol, Visa Intelligent Commerce, into OpenAI's large language models, allowing AI agents to execute tokenized transactions over Visa's network on behalf of users. The move was announced alongside Mastercard, which launched 'Agent Pay for Machines' with 30 partners, as both networks race to position their rails for autonomous, AI-driven purchases.
items.whyItMatters: Positions Visa to capture transaction volume as commerce shifts into AI chat interfaces, defending its network from being disintermediated by agent-led checkout.

items.headline: DBS to launch retail tokenized physical gold, each token backed by one gram of vaulted bullion
items.brief: On June 11, 2026, DBS — Singapore's largest bank by assets — said it will offer DBS Physical Gold Tokens to retail customers via its digibank app in the second half of 2026. Each token represents one gram of gold (about S$200, or ~$155, at announcement), is backed by bullion in a dedicated Singapore vault, and can be traded around the clock and redeemed for physical metal. DBS calls it the first offering in Singapore letting retail customers access, hold and trade tokenized physical gold on a single platform.
items.whyItMatters: Extends DBS's digital-asset franchise into mass-market retail and follows rival OCBC's April gold-token launch, intensifying competition in Singapore tokenized assets.

items.headline: CME Group to launch four new E-mini index futures on June 29 covering 90%+ of U.S. investable market cap
items.brief: On June 11, 2026, CME Group said it will list four new E-mini broad-market futures on June 29: E-mini Morningstar U.S. Total Market, Russell 3000, S&P 1500 Composite, and S&P Total Market index futures. CME said the contracts let participants trade futures on indices covering more than 90% of the entire U.S. investable market capitalization, broadening its equity-index suite.
items.whyItMatters: Expands CME's flagship equity-index franchise into total-market exposure, adding fee-generating products in its highest-volume segment.

items.headline: Sea's Shopee cuts hundreds of developers — ~8% of its dev workforce — in pivot to AI
items.brief: Sea Limited is eliminating several hundred developer roles at Shopee, roughly 8% of the e-commerce unit's development workforce, with quality-assurance and general engineering positions hit and further cuts possible. The move follows CEO Forrest Li's declaration that a trillion-dollar valuation is achievable by doubling down on AI, with Shopee embedding AI into product recommendations and seller tools and partnering with Google on AI shopping agents. A Sea spokesperson said affected staff would receive support during the transition.
items.whyItMatters: Signals a structural cost-and-headcount reset at Sea's largest segment as it reallocates toward AI; watch whether margins improve without denting Shopee's ~25% GMV growth target.

items.title: ROIC (Return on Invested Capital)
items.body: Il ROIC misura quanto rende il capitale realmente messo al lavoro nell'azienda, sia equity che debito. In parole semplici: profitto operativo dopo le tasse diviso il capitale investito (debito + patrimonio netto, meno la cassa in eccesso). È più onesto del ROE perché non si lascia gonfiare dal debito: un'azienda può sembrare brillante col ROE solo perché si è indebitata molto. Da investitore retail, confronta il ROIC con il costo del capitale: se il ROIC è stabilmente sopra (es. 15% vs un costo dell'8%), l'azienda crea valore ogni volta che reinveste.
items.example: Profitto operativo netto 200, capitale investito 1.000 → ROIC = 20%. Se prendere a prestito le costa l'8%, ogni euro reinvestito a 20% genera valore.
items.whyItMatters: È il singolo numero che distingue le aziende che creano valore reinvestendo da quelle che lo distruggono.

items.title: Liquidation preference
items.body: La liquidation preference è la clausola che decide chi viene pagato per primo, e quanto, quando una startup viene venduta o liquidata. Gli investitori ottengono di riavere il loro capitale (a volte 1x, a volte un multiplo) prima che i fondatori e i dipendenti vedano un euro. Con una '1x non-participating' l'investitore sceglie tra riprendersi i soldi o convertire in azioni; con una 'participating' prima riprende i soldi e poi partecipa anche al resto — molto più punitiva per i fondatori. Conta perché un'exit che sembra grande sulla carta può lasciare ai fondatori molto meno del previsto.
items.example: Investitore mette 10M con 1x participating. L'azienda è venduta a 30M: lui prende prima i 10M, poi una quota dei 20M rimanenti — non solo la sua percentuale secca.
items.whyItMatters: Determina chi guadagna davvero in un'exit, spesso più della percentuale di possesso.

items.title: Momento angolare
items.body: Il momento angolare è la 'quantità di rotazione' di un oggetto che gira, e in assenza di forze esterne si conserva: non può sparire, deve restare costante. Dipende da quanto è veloce la rotazione e da quanto la massa è distribuita lontano dal centro. Se avvicini la massa al centro, per mantenere costante il totale la rotazione deve accelerare. È lo stesso principio che tiene in piedi una trottola e che fa restare orientata la Terra nel suo asse.
items.example: La pattinatrice sul ghiaccio che gira: apre le braccia e rallenta, le chiude al petto e accelera vorticosamente. Non ha spinto nulla — ha solo riavvicinato la massa all'asse.
items.whyItMatters: Spiega trottole, giroscopi, satelliti e perfino la stabilità di una bicicletta in movimento.

items.title: Sfumato di Leonardo
items.body: Lo sfumato è la tecnica con cui Leonardo da Vinci eliminava i contorni netti, facendo passare luce e ombra con transizioni così graduali da sembrare fumo nell'aria (da qui il nome). Invece di disegnare una linea attorno a un volto, sovrapponeva veli sottilissimi di colore semitrasparente, costruendo i passaggi a strati. Il risultato è morbidezza e profondità: la figura sembra respirare e fondersi con l'atmosfera. Guarda gli angoli della bocca e degli occhi della Gioconda — non c'è un bordo preciso, ed è proprio quell'ambiguità a renderne l'espressione indecifrabile.
items.example: Nella Gioconda il sorriso 'cambia' a seconda di dove guardi: gli angoli sfumati lasciano al tuo occhio il compito di completarlo.
items.whyItMatters: È il trucco tecnico dietro l'espressione più enigmatica della storia dell'arte.

items.title: Il container che ha creato la globalizzazione
items.body: Nel 1956 l'imprenditore americano Malcom McLean fece partire la prima nave con container standardizzati. Prima, caricare e scaricare una nave significava centinaia di scaricatori che spostavano a mano sacchi e casse per giorni, e il costo del trasporto poteva essere una grossa fetta del valore della merce. La scatola metallica standard, sollevabile da una gru e trasferibile direttamente su camion e treno, fece crollare quel costo quasi a zero. Questo, più dei trattati commerciali, rese economicamente sensato produrre in Asia e vendere ovunque: senza container, non esisterebbe la catena di fornitura globale che conosciamo.
items.example: Scaricare manualmente una nave costava intorno ai 5–6 dollari a tonnellata negli anni '50; col container scese a pochi centesimi.
items.whyItMatters: La fabbrica del mondo in Asia esiste perché spostare merci è diventato quasi gratis.

items.title: L'inversione di Charlie Munger
items.body: Munger ripeteva un consiglio rubato al matematico Jacobi: 'Inverti, sempre inverti'. Invece di chiederti solo come ottenere ciò che vuoi, chiediti cosa garantirebbe il fallimento — e poi evita accuratamente quelle cose. È spesso più facile e affidabile identificare gli errori da non commettere che progettare la strada perfetta verso il successo. Eliminando sistematicamente i modi sicuri di fallire, quello che resta tende a funzionare.
items.example: Vuoi una vita felice? Munger la girava: elenca cosa rende la vita miserabile (invidia, risentimento, spese fuori controllo, inaffidabilità) ed evita quelle. Cosa resta è quasi automaticamente una buona vita.
items.whyItMatters: Evitare l'errore stupido batte spesso l'inseguire la mossa geniale.

items.title: Perché la litografia EUV è così difficile
items.body: I chip più avanzati si 'stampano' proiettando luce attraverso una maschera su una fetta di silicio: più corta è la lunghezza d'onda della luce, più piccoli e fitti i transistor che puoi disegnare. L'EUV usa luce ultravioletta estrema a 13,5 nanometri, ma quella luce viene assorbita da quasi tutto, persino dall'aria e dal vetro normale. Per questo non si possono usare lenti tradizionali: serve il vuoto e specchi perfetti al livello atomico. La luce stessa si genera colpendo goccioline di stagno con un laser potentissimo, decine di migliaia di volte al secondo. È così complesso che al mondo una sola azienda, l'olandese ASML, sa costruire queste macchine.
items.example: Una macchina EUV pesa come un autobus, costa intorno ai 150–200 milioni di dollari e si spedisce in centinaia di casse.
items.whyItMatters: Spiega perché il potere sui chip avanzati si concentra in pochissime aziende e diventa leva geopolitica.

items.title: Allenare il VO2max con gli intervalli 4x4
items.body: Il VO2max è la massima quantità di ossigeno che il corpo riesce a usare sotto sforzo, ed è uno dei migliori predittori sia di prestazione di endurance sia di longevità. Per alzarlo serve passare abbastanza tempo vicino al massimo, cosa difficile da fare in un colpo solo: il protocollo norvegese 4x4 risolve il problema spezzandolo. Fai 4 minuti molto duri (circa 90-95% della frequenza cardiaca massima, dove riesci a malapena a dire una frase) seguiti da 3 minuti di recupero attivo blando, ripetuti 4 volte. Per un atleta HYROX è un investimento diretto sul 'motore' che regge i blocchi di corsa tra le stazioni.
items.example: Così lo applichi: 1-2 sessioni 4x4 a settimana (su tapis roulant, bici o remoergometro), tenendo facile il resto del volume per recuperare — la qualità nei 4 minuti vale più della quantità.
items.whyItMatters: Pochi intervalli mirati a settimana spostano l'indicatore più legato a performance e anni di vita in salute.

lesson.title: I quattro casi tedeschi: la mappa di tutto il corso
lesson.markdown:
# I quattro casi tedeschi: la mappa di tutto il corso Benvenuto/a alla prima lezione di **Grammatica tedesca**. Diamo per acquisiti alfabeto, pronuncia e presente di base: partiamo dal cuore vero della lingua, quello su cui costruiremo tutto il resto del corso — il **sistema dei casi** (*das Kasussystem*). In italiano l'ordine delle parole ti dice chi fa cosa: *"Il cane morde l'uomo"* è diverso da *"L'uomo morde il cane"*. In tedesco è la **forma dell'articolo** (e della desinenza) a dirtelo, non la posizione. Capire i casi significa quindi capire *come il tedesco segna i ruoli* dentro la frase. ## I quattro casi e a cosa servono Ogni gruppo nominale in tedesco sta in uno dei quattro casi. La domanda-guida è il tuo strumento più affidabile: | Caso | Ruolo | Domanda | Esempio IT | |------|-------|---------|------------| | **Nominativ** | soggetto | *wer/was?* (chi/cosa?) | *il cane* dorme | | **Akkusativ** | oggetto diretto | *wen/was?* (chi/che cosa?) | vedo *il cane* | | **Dativ** | oggetto indiretto | *wem?* (a chi?) | do un osso *al cane* | | **Genitiv** | possesso/relazione | *wessen?* (di chi?) | la coda *del cane* | > 💡 Il **Nominativ** è il caso "di partenza", quello che trovi nel dizionario. Tutti gli altri sono *trasformazioni* a partire da lì. ## La tabella madre: l'articolo determinativo Questa tabella è la più importante dell'intero corso. Memorizzala: la richiameremo in ogni lezione su aggettivi, preposizioni e pronomi relativi. | | maschile (m) | femminile (f) | neutro (n) | plurale (pl) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Nom.** | **der** | **die** | **das** | **die** | | **Akk.** | **den** | **die** | **das** | **die** | | **Dat.** | **dem** | **der** | **dem** | **den** + *-n* | | **Gen.** | **des** + *-s* | **der** | **des** + *-s* | **der** | Tre osservazioni che ti salvano la vita: 1. **Solo il maschile cambia all'Akkusativ**: *der → **den***. Femminile, neutro e plurale restano identici al Nominativo. È l'errore #1 da evitare. 2. Al **Dativ plurale** il sostantivo prende una *-n* finale: *die Kinder → mit **den Kindern*** (*con i bambini*). 3. Al **Genitiv** i sostantivi maschili e neutri aggiungono **-s** (o **-es**): *das Auto → des **Autos*** (*dell'automobile*). ## L'articolo indeterminativo e la negazione *ein/kein* e i possessivi (*mein, dein, sein*…) seguono lo **stesso schema di desinenze**, ma al Nominativo maschile/neutro l'articolo è "nudo" (senza finale): | | m | f | n | pl (*kein*) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Nom.** | **ein** | **eine** | **ein** | **keine** | | **Akk.** | **einen** | **eine** | **ein** | **keine** | | **Dat.** | **einem** | **einer** | **einem** | **keinen** | | **Gen.** | **eines** | **einer** | **eines** | **keiner** | *ein* non ha plurale; per "nessuno/non…" usi **kein**, che invece il plurale ce l'ha. ## Vederli lavorare insieme Una sola frase con tutti i ruoli: > **Der Mann** gibt **dem Kind einen Apfel** wegen **des Geburtstags**. > *L'uomo dà al bambino una mela a causa del compleanno.* - **Der Mann** = chi dà? → **Nominativ** (soggetto) - **dem Kind** = a chi? → **Dativ** (destinatario) - **einen Apfel** = che cosa? → **Akkusativ** (oggetto diretto, *ein → einen* perché *Apfel* è maschile) - **des Geburtstags** = relazione retta da *wegen* → **Genitiv** Nota come l'**ordine** classico sia *soggetto – verbo – Dativ – Akkusativ*, ma è la *forma* a fissare i ruoli: potrei spostare i complementi senza creare ambiguità. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Wer/Was?
NOMINATIV
(soggetto)"] --> V((Verbo)) V --> B["Wen/Was?
AKKUSATIV
(ogg. diretto)"] V --> C["Wem?
DATIV
(ogg. indiretto)"] A -.->|"Wessen?"| D["GENITIV
(di chi?)"] ``` ## Errori tipici - ❌ *Ich sehe **der** Mann.* → ✅ *Ich sehe **den** Mann.* (*Vedo l'uomo* — Akkusativ maschile!) - ❌ *mit **die** Freunden* → ✅ *mit **den** Freunden* (Dativ plurale: articolo *den* **e** sostantivo in *-n*). - ❌ Tradurre l'italiano *"a"* sempre con una preposizione: in tedesco *"do **a** lui"* è spesso solo il **Dativ** senza preposizione → *Ich gebe **ihm**…* ## Esercizi **1.** Indica il caso e la domanda-guida di ogni parte in grassetto: *a)* **Die Lehrerin** erklärt **den Schülern** **die Grammatik**. **2.** Completa con l'articolo determinativo corretto: *a)* Ich kaufe ___ Buch (n, Akk). *b)* Wir helfen ___ Frau (f, Dat). *c)* Das ist das Auto ___ Mannes (m, Gen). **3.** Correggi l'errore: *Er gibt **die** Hund **ein** Knochen.* (*Dà un osso al cane*; *Hund* = m, *Knochen* = m) **4.** Traduci in tedesco: *Il bambino (das Kind) dà alla madre (die Mutter) un fiore (die Blume).* ### Soluzioni **1.** *Die Lehrerin* = Nominativ (*wer?*); *den Schülern* = Dativ (*wem?*, + *-n* al plurale); *die Grammatik* = Akkusativ (*was?*). **2.** *a)* **das** Buch; *b)* **der** Frau; *c)* **des** Mannes. **3.** *Er gibt **dem** Hund **einen** Knochen.* (*Hund* è il destinatario → Dativ *dem*; *Knochen* è l'oggetto diretto maschile → Akkusativ *einen*). **4.** *Das Kind gibt **der** Mutter **eine** Blume.* (*der Mutter* = Dativ; *eine Blume* = Akkusativ femminile, identico al Nominativo). ## Takeaways - In tedesco i **ruoli** li segnano i **casi** (forma dell'articolo/desinenza), non l'ordine delle parole. - Quattro casi, quattro domande: **Nominativ** *wer/was*, **Akkusativ** *wen/was*, **Dativ** *wem*, **Genitiv** *wessen*. - La **tabella di *der/die/das*** è la chiave maestra del corso: ricordala a memoria. - Trappole d'oro: solo il **maschile** cambia all'Akkusativ (*der → den*); il **Dativ plurale** aggiunge *-n* al sostantivo; maschile/neutro al **Genitiv** prendono *-s*. - Prossima lezione: useremo questa tabella per affrontare la **declinazione dell'aggettivo** (forte, debole, mista).

lesson.title: Die professionelle E-Mail: Aufbau und Anrede
lesson.markdown:
# Die professionelle E-Mail: Aufbau und Anrede

Benvenuto/a alla prima lezione di **Business German**. Diamo per acquisite le basi (sai presentarti, conosci il presente e il *Perfekt*): partiamo subito dalla situazione più frequente in ufficio, l'**E-Mail** (*email*). In tedesco la corrispondenza professionale è sorprendentemente formalizzata: esistono formule fisse che *si usano e basta*. Impararle ti fa suonare immediatamente competente.

## La struttura standard

Ogni email formale tedesca segue uno schema rigido. Vediamolo dall'alto verso il basso.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Betreff (oggetto)"] --> B["Anrede (formula di apertura)"]
    B --> C["Einleitung (frase introduttiva)"]
    C --> D["Hauptteil (corpo del messaggio)"]
    D --> E["Schluss (chiusura / call to action)"]
    E --> F["Grußformel (saluto finale)"]
    F --> G["Signatur (firma)"]
```

I termini chiave:

- **der Betreff** — *l'oggetto*
- **die Anrede** — *la formula di apertura* (il "Gentile…")
- **die Grußformel** — *la formula di saluto finale*
- **die Anlage / der Anhang** — *l'allegato*
- **der Empfänger** — *il destinatario* / **der Absender** — *il mittente*

## L'Anrede: la regola d'oro

La formula di apertura più sicura, sempre corretta nel registro formale, è:

> **Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren,** — *Egregie Signore, Egregi Signori,*

Si usa quando **non conosci il nome** del destinatario. Se invece lo conosci, personalizza:

| Situazione | Tedesco | Italiano |
|---|---|---|
| Uomo, nome noto | **Sehr geehrter Herr Müller,** | *Egregio Signor Müller,* |
| Donna, nome noto | **Sehr geehrte Frau Schmidt,** | *Gentile Signora Schmidt,* |
| Più persone (nomi noti) | **Sehr geehrte Frau Schmidt, sehr geehrter Herr Müller,** | *Gentile Sig.ra…, Egregio Sig.…* |
| Rapporto già consolidato | **Liebe Frau Schmidt,** / **Lieber Herr Müller,** | *Cara/Caro…* |

> ⚠️ **Attenzione a tre dettagli:**
> 1. Dopo l'*Anrede* va **la virgola**, non i due punti (come fa invece l'inglese).
> 2. La prima parola del corpo dopo la virgola è in **minuscolo** (a meno che non sia un sostantivo o "Sie").
> 3. **Geehrter** ha la *-r* (maschile), **geehrte** no (femminile/plurale). È un aggettivo declinato: la desinenza segue il genere del destinatario.

Da evitare nel formale: *Hallo* o *Hi* (troppo informali con sconosciuti) e — errore classico degli italofoni — il calco "*Sehr geehrte Herr*" senza la *-r*.

## Le Redemittel di apertura e chiusura

Ecco i mattoni pronti all'uso. Memorizzali come blocchi.

**Frase introduttiva (Einleitung):**

- **Ich schreibe Ihnen bezüglich …** — *Le scrivo in merito a …*
- **vielen Dank für Ihre E-Mail vom 10. Juni.** — *molte grazie per la Sua email del 10 giugno.*
- **Ich beziehe mich auf unser Telefonat von gestern.** — *Faccio riferimento alla nostra telefonata di ieri.*

**Corpo / richiesta (Hauptteil):**

- **Könnten Sie mir bitte … zusenden?** — *Potrebbe inviarmi per cortesia …?*
- **Ich möchte Sie darüber informieren, dass …** — *Desidero informarLa che …*
- **Im Anhang finden Sie …** — *In allegato trova …*

**Chiusura (Schluss):**

- **Für Rückfragen stehe ich Ihnen gerne zur Verfügung.** — *Per ulteriori domande resto volentieri a Sua disposizione.*
- **Ich freue mich auf Ihre Rückmeldung.** — *Attendo con piacere un Suo riscontro.*

**Grußformel (saluto finale):**

> **Mit freundlichen Grüßen** — *Cordiali saluti* (lo standard assoluto, spesso abbreviato **MfG** ma in forma estesa è più elegante)

Variante leggermente più calda, ottima per chi già conosci: **Beste Grüße** / **Viele Grüße** (*Cari saluti*). Dopo la *Grußformel* **non si mette la virgola**.

## Testo-modello

Ecco un'email completa che richiede un preventivo (anticipiamo il tema *Angebot*, che vedremo a fondo più avanti):

```text
Betreff: Anfrage Angebot – Büromöbel

Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren,

ich schreibe Ihnen bezüglich Ihres Katalogs für Büromöbel.
Unser Unternehmen plant die Einrichtung eines neuen
Standorts in München.

Könnten Sie mir bitte ein Angebot für zehn höhenverstellbare
Schreibtische zusenden? Im Anhang finden Sie unsere
genauen Anforderungen.

Für Rückfragen stehe ich Ihnen gerne zur Verfügung.
Ich freue mich auf Ihre Rückmeldung.

Mit freundlichen Grüßen
Andrea Rossi
Einkaufsleiter, Rossi GmbH
```

Glossario del testo:
- **die Anfrage** — *la richiesta (di informazioni/preventivo)*
- **das Unternehmen** — *l'azienda*
- **der Standort** — *la sede/filiale*
- **höhenverstellbar** — *regolabile in altezza*
- **der Schreibtisch** — *la scrivania*
- **der Einkaufsleiter** — *il responsabile acquisti*

## Nota di registro: il "Sie"

In tutta la corrispondenza professionale si usa il **Siezen**, cioè dare del "Lei": pronome **Sie** e possessivo **Ihr** (*Suo/Vostro*), entrambi **sempre con la maiuscola** nelle email. Passare al **du** (*tu*) avviene solo dopo un esplicito invito — spesso la formula **„Wir können uns gerne duzen."** (*Possiamo darci del tu, se Le fa piacere.*). Finché ciò non accade, mantieni il *Sie*: in Germania la formalità non è freddezza, è rispetto professionale di default.

## Takeaways

- Lo scheletro di un'email tedesca è fisso: **Betreff → Anrede → Einleitung → Hauptteil → Schluss → Grußformel → Signatur**.
- Apertura universale e sicura: **Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren,** — con **virgola** e prima parola **minuscola**.
- Ricorda la declinazione: **geehrte_r_ Herr** (maschile) vs **geehrte Frau** (femminile).
- Chiusura standard: **Mit freundlichen Grüßen**, *senza* virgola dopo.
- Usa sempre il **Sie / Ihr** (maiuscoli) finché non ti viene proposto il *du*.
- Impara le *Redemittel* come blocchi pronti: ti fanno scrivere veloce e suonare nativo.

lesson.title: Lesson 1: Thinking About Scale — Latency, Throughput, and the Two Ways to Grow
lesson.markdown:
# Lesson 1: Thinking About Scale — Latency, Throughput, and the Two Ways to Grow Welcome to **System Design**. Over this course you'll learn to reason about systems that serve millions of users without falling over: load balancers, caches, sharded databases, queues, and the trade-offs that bind them. But before any of that, you need the *vocabulary of performance* and the *first strategic choice* every architect faces. That's today. ## Why "design" at all? A program that works on your laptop for one user is an engineering problem. The same program serving 10 million users is a *systems* problem — the bottleneck is rarely your code's cleverness and almost always a **resource limit**: CPU, memory, disk I/O, network bandwidth, or a database connection pool. System design is the discipline of arranging components so that no single resource becomes a wall. To talk about walls, we need two numbers. ## Latency vs. Throughput These are the two most-confused terms in the field. They are *not* the same, and optimizing one can hurt the other. - **Latency** is the time to complete *one* operation — how long a single request waits for its answer. Measured in milliseconds (ms). *"How fast?"* - **Throughput** is the number of operations completed *per unit time* — requests per second (RPS), queries per second (QPS). *"How many?"* > A highway analogy: **latency** is how long it takes *your* car to drive from A to B. **Throughput** is how many cars *cross the bridge* per minute. Adding lanes (throughput) doesn't make your individual trip faster; raising the speed limit (latency) doesn't necessarily move more cars if there's a toll booth. The relationship is captured by **Little's Law**, one of the few exact laws in this field: $$L = \lambda \times W$$ where $L$ is the average number of requests *in the system concurrently*, $\lambda$ is the arrival rate (throughput), and $W$ is the average time each request spends in the system (latency). Rearranged, $\lambda = L / W$: to raise throughput you must either handle more requests concurrently ($L$) or make each one faster ($W$). ### Tail latency matters more than the average A critical subtlety: never trust the *average* latency. If 99% of requests take 10 ms but 1% take 2 seconds, a user clicking through 100 pages will almost certainly hit that slow path. We therefore measure **percentiles**: | Metric | Meaning | |--------|---------| | **p50** (median) | half of requests are faster than this | | **p95** | 95% are faster; the "typical bad case" | | **p99** | the **tail** — what your unhappiest users feel | > **Rule of thumb:** at scale, *tail latency is the user experience.* Optimizing p99 is usually the real goal. A few latency numbers worth internalizing (orders of magnitude, not exact): | Operation | Approx. time | |-----------|-------------| | L1 cache reference | ~1 ns | | Main memory (RAM) read | ~100 ns | | SSD random read | ~16 µs | | Network round trip, same datacenter | ~0.5 ms | | Disk (HDD) seek | ~10 ms | | Network round trip, cross-continent | ~150 ms | Notice memory is roughly **100,000× faster** than a cross-continent round trip. This single gap is *why caching and CDNs exist* — later lessons. ## The first strategic choice: scaling up vs. scaling out When a server hits its limit, you have exactly two moves. **Vertical scaling (scale up):** make the machine bigger — more CPU cores, more RAM, faster disks. Simple, no code changes, no distributed-systems complexity. But it has a hard ceiling (the biggest machine money can buy), gets exponentially expensive at the top end, and that one machine is a **single point of failure**. **Horizontal scaling (scale out):** add *more* machines and spread the work across them. Effectively unlimited headroom, and redundancy is built in — if one node dies, others carry on. The cost: you now have a *distributed system*. You need a way to route requests (a **load balancer**), and your application must ideally be **stateless** so any machine can serve any request. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Vertical["Vertical scaling — scale UP"] S1["Small server
4 CPU / 16GB"] --> S2["Bigger server
64 CPU / 512GB"] end subgraph Horizontal["Horizontal scaling — scale OUT"] LB(["Load balancer"]) --> A["Server A"] LB --> B["Server B"] LB --> C["Server C"] end ``` | | Vertical (up) | Horizontal (out) | |---|---|---| | Complexity | Low | High (distributed) | | Ceiling | Hardware limit | ~Unlimited | | Fault tolerance | Single point of failure | Redundant | | Cost curve | Exponential at top | Roughly linear | | App changes | None | Statelessness, routing | > **The practitioner's path:** start vertical because it's cheap and simple. Scale out *before* you hit the ceiling — and design for statelessness early so the transition isn't a rewrite. This choice cascades through the entire course: the moment you go horizontal, you need load balancing (Lesson 2), distributed caching (Lesson 3), and eventually database sharding and replication — which forces the consistency trade-offs of CAP. Every later topic is, in some sense, a consequence of choosing to scale out. ## Takeaways - **Latency** = time per operation; **throughput** = operations per unit time. They are distinct and can trade off against each other. - **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$) ties them together: more concurrency or lower latency are your only levers on throughput. - Measure **percentiles (p95/p99)**, not averages — *tail latency is the user experience*. - Memorize the latency orders of magnitude: RAM is ~100,000× faster than a cross-continent round trip — the reason caches and CDNs exist. - **Vertical scaling** is simple but capped and fragile; **horizontal scaling** is unbounded and fault-tolerant but demands a load balancer and stateless design. - Start simple, scale out before the ceiling, and design statelessness in early. *Next lesson: **Load Balancing** — how to spread traffic across those horizontal machines, and the algorithms that decide who serves what.*

lesson.title: What Is a Language Model? From Text to Probabilities to Tokens
lesson.markdown:
# What Is a Language Model? From Text to Probabilities to Tokens

Welcome to **LLM & AI Engineering**. Over this course we'll build up, brick by brick, from the most basic question — *what is a language model?* — all the way to deploying, evaluating, and optimizing real systems. Today we lay the foundation. By the end of this lesson you'll understand what a language model actually *computes*, and why text must first be broken into **tokens**.

## A language model is a probability machine

Strip away the hype and a **language model (LM)** is one thing: a function that assigns a **probability** to a sequence of text. Given some text so far, it predicts *what comes next*.

Formally, a language model defines a probability distribution over the next token given all previous tokens. If a sequence is $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T$, the model factorizes the probability of the whole sequence using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_1, \dots, x_{t-1})$$

Read this carefully, because it's the heart of everything:

- Each factor $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ is the probability of the $t$-th token **given everything before it**.
- The model only ever has to answer one question, over and over: *given this context, what's the next token?*
- Multiplying these conditional probabilities reconstructs the probability of any sequence.

A model trained this way — predicting the next token from the left context — is called an **autoregressive** language model. Every LLM you've heard of (GPT, Claude, Llama) is autoregressive at its core.

> **Key insight:** An LLM doesn't "know facts" the way a database does. It has learned a staggeringly rich estimate of $P(\text{next token} \mid \text{context})$. *Generation* is just repeatedly sampling from this distribution and feeding the result back in.

### Generation is a loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Context: tokens so far] --> B[Model computes P over vocabulary]
    B --> C[Pick next token e.g. sample or argmax]
    C --> D[Append token to context]
    D --> A
    C --> E{Stop token or limit?}
    E -- no --> A
    E -- yes --> F[Done]
```

This loop is why generation feels like "thinking out loud": the model commits to one token, then reconsiders the whole future given that choice. We'll return to the mechanics of *picking* the token (temperature, top-p) in the inference lessons.

## Why not just predict words?

Naively, you might model text word-by-word. But natural language is messy:

- **Vocabulary explosion.** English alone has hundreds of thousands of words, plus names, typos, code, emoji, and other languages. A fixed word list can never cover them.
- **Out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words.** What probability do you assign to a word you've never seen, like *"antidisestablishmentarianismify"*? With words, you're stuck.
- **Wasted structure.** "run", "runs", "running", "runner" share a root, but a word model treats them as unrelated atoms.

The fix is to model text at a level *between* characters and words. That unit is the **token**.

## Tokens: the real atoms of an LLM

A **token** is a chunk of text — often a whole common word, sometimes a sub-word fragment, sometimes a single character or byte. The process of converting raw text into tokens is **tokenization**, and the algorithm that does it is the **tokenizer**.

Modern LLMs typically use **subword tokenization**, most commonly **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**. The idea is elegantly simple:

1. Start with a base vocabulary of individual bytes/characters.
2. Scan a large corpus and find the **most frequent adjacent pair** of symbols.
3. **Merge** that pair into a new single token; add it to the vocabulary.
4. Repeat thousands of times until you reach a target vocabulary size (e.g. ~50k–200k tokens).

The result: frequent words become a single token, while rare words gracefully decompose into known sub-pieces. Nothing is ever truly OOV, because you can always fall back to bytes.

Here's roughly how a sentence might split (using `·` to mark token boundaries, where `▁` denotes a leading space):

```text
"Tokenization isn't magic."
→ ▁Token | ization | ▁isn | 't | ▁magic | .
```

Notice the tradeoffs at work:

| Granularity | Vocab size | Sequence length | OOV problem |
|-------------|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| Characters  | Tiny      | Very long       | None, but inefficient |
| **Subwords (BPE)** | **Medium** | **Moderate** | **Essentially none** |
| Words       | Huge      | Short           | Severe |

Subwords sit in the sweet spot: a manageable vocabulary, reasonable sequence lengths, and no OOV cliff.

### Why tokens matter to *you* as an engineer

This isn't academic — tokens are the unit of nearly everything practical:

- **Cost.** APIs bill *per token*, input and output. Knowing that "Hello, world!" is ~4 tokens, not 13 characters or 2 words, lets you estimate spend.
- **Context window.** A model's limit (e.g. 200k tokens) is measured in tokens, not words. As a rule of thumb in English, **1 token ≈ 4 characters ≈ ¾ of a word**, so ~1,000 tokens ≈ 750 words.
- **Behavior quirks.** Because the model sees tokens, not letters, it can struggle to count the *r*'s in "strawberry" — the word may be a single opaque token. Many "dumb LLM" moments are really *tokenization* moments.

> **Try this mentally:** "ChatGPT" might be `Chat | G | PT` — three tokens. The model never sees the clean word "ChatGPT" the way you do.

## Where we go next

We now have the spine of the whole course: **text → tokens → next-token probabilities → generation**. But we've skipped a magic step. The model can't multiply *symbols* — it needs *numbers*. How does the token `▁magic` become something a neural network can compute with? That's **embeddings**, our next lesson.

## Takeaways

- A **language model** estimates $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ — the probability of the next token given prior context; generation is sampling from it in a loop.
- LLMs are **autoregressive**: they factor a sequence's probability via the **chain rule** and predict one token at a time.
- **Tokens**, not words or characters, are the true atoms. **Subword tokenization (BPE)** balances vocabulary size, sequence length, and the OOV problem.
- Tokens drive the practicals you'll live with: **cost**, **context windows** (~1 token ≈ ¾ word in English), and odd failures like miscounting letters.
- Next stop: turning tokens into vectors — **embeddings**.
